IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important problem—context-aware vertex representations that combine structural and textual signals—and demonstrates measurable gains on link prediction on standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) compared to common baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE). The mutual attention mechanism and concatenation of structure/text embeddings offer a practical modeling approach that could influence follow-up work, but the impact is somewhat limited by lack of code availability, no reported broader evaluations (e.g., more diverse networks or downstream tasks), and only comparable performance on vertex classification. Overall, the contribution is useful and likely to be built upon by researchers in network embedding, but it is not clearly transformative. | B(3): The paper targets an important problem—learning context-aware vertex embeddings that vary by interaction—and proposes a concrete mechanism (concatenated structure/text embeddings with a mutual attention text module) that yields clear gains on link prediction. However, the evaluation is limited to standard small datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and improvements are mixed (significant for link prediction but only comparable for vertex classification), so the work appears useful and practically applicable but not likely to fundamentally shift current thinking. It does suggest viable follow-ups (contextualized graph representations and attention-based text integration), so it opens moderate new directions without being transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—context-aware vertex representations that combine structural and textual signals—and demonstrates measurable gains on link prediction on standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) compared to common baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE). The mutual attention mechanism and concatenation of structure/text embeddings offer a practical modeling approach that could influence follow-up work, but the impact is somewhat limited by lack of code availability, no reported broader evaluations (e.g., more diverse networks or downstream tasks), and only comparable performance on vertex classification. Overall, the contribution is useful and likely to be built upon by researchers in network embedding, but it is not clearly transformative.

However, the evaluation is limited to standard small datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and improvements are mixed (significant for link prediction but only comparable for vertex classification), so the work appears useful and practically applicable but not likely to fundamentally shift current thinking. It does suggest viable follow-ups (contextualized graph representations and attention-based text integration), so it opens moderate new directions without being transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses network embedding with context-aware text+structure modeling and a mutual-attention mechanism, which is squarely within top ML/NLP venue scope. Evaluation uses standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu), strong baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE), and common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) with reported hyperparameters, matching expected methodological standards. From a reproducibility standpoint I note missing mention of released code and limited dataset diversity/statistical analysis, which are minor concerns for a top-tier venue but do not preclude relevance. Overall the work targets the right audience and problems, though greater emphasis on reproducibility and broader evaluation would strengthen fit. | B(4): This paper sits squarely in the network representation learning area and combines structure and text via a mutual attention mechanism, which is highly relevant to top ML/NLP venues that value representation and text-enhanced graph models. The problem formulation, use of standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu), baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE), and evaluation metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) match community expectations. My only minor concern is positioning relative to rapidly advancing graph neural models and whether the novelty over recent context-aware/attention-based graph methods is sufficient, but overall the work targets the right audience and standards for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses network embedding with context-aware text+structure modeling and a mutual-attention mechanism, which is squarely within top ML/NLP venue scope. Evaluation uses standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu), strong baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE), and common metrics (AUC, classification accuracy) with reported hyperparameters, matching expected methodological standards. From a reproducibility standpoint I note missing mention of released code and limited dataset diversity/statistical analysis, which are minor concerns for a top-tier venue but do not preclude relevance. Overall the work targets the right audience and problems, though greater emphasis on reproducibility and broader evaluation would strengthen fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the network representation learning area and combines structure and text via a mutual attention mechanism, which is highly relevant to top ML/NLP venues that value representation and text-enhanced graph models. My only minor concern is positioning relative to rapidly advancing graph neural models and whether the novelty over recent context-aware/attention-based graph methods is sufficient, but overall the work targets the right audience and standards for a top conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): From the summary, CANE's main novelty is producing context-aware, interaction-dependent vertex embeddings and applying a mutual attention mechanism to fuse text and structure representations. While the context-aware perspective and attention-based fusion are reasonable extensions beyond baseline methods like TADW and CENE, they appear as an incremental combination of known ideas (structure+text concatenation, negative sampling, Adam optimization) rather than a fundamentally new formulation. The paper differentiates its contributions from prior work by emphasizing mutual attention for semantic relations, which would be informative to experts but not a highly surprising leap. | B(3): CANE contributes a moderately novel combination: producing context-dependent vertex embeddings per interaction and incorporating a mutual attention mechanism to fuse text and structure. The problem (network embedding with text) is well-studied (e.g., TADW, CENE), and CANE’s main novelty lies in the context-aware formulation and the mutual attention rather than a radically new problem or learning paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates itself by emphasizing per-interaction embeddings and reports improvements on link prediction, but the methodological advances appear to be non-trivial extensions of existing text-augmented and attention-based embedding techniques rather than a highly original departure that would surprise experts.

reviewer_a.justification: From the summary, CANE's main novelty is producing context-aware, interaction-dependent vertex embeddings and applying a mutual attention mechanism to fuse text and structure representations. While the context-aware perspective and attention-based fusion are reasonable extensions beyond baseline methods like TADW and CENE, they appear as an incremental combination of known ideas (structure+text concatenation, negative sampling, Adam optimization) rather than a fundamentally new formulation. The paper differentiates its contributions from prior work by emphasizing mutual attention for semantic relations, which would be informative to experts but not a highly surprising leap.

reviewer_b.justification: CANE contributes a moderately novel combination: producing context-dependent vertex embeddings per interaction and incorporating a mutual attention mechanism to fuse text and structure. The problem (network embedding with text) is well-studied (e.g., TADW, CENE), and CANE’s main novelty lies in the context-aware formulation and the mutual attention rather than a radically new problem or learning paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates itself by emphasizing per-interaction embeddings and reports improvements on link prediction, but the methodological advances appear to be non-trivial extensions of existing text-augmented and attention-based embedding techniques rather than a highly original departure that would surprise experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear main claims and provides experimental evidence showing significant link-prediction improvements and comparable node-classification performance on Cora, HepTh and Zhihu against reasonable baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE). However, from the summary there is no sign of ablation studies isolating the mutual-attention or concatenation design choices, no statistical significance testing, and no mention of released code or runtime/memory measurements to ensure reproducibility. Mathematical details appear minimal in the summary (no proofs), and some assumptions (e.g., benefit of concatenation vs. fusion) are not directly supported by controlled experiments, so the technical claims are plausible but not fully substantiated. | B(4): The paper states clear technical claims about producing context-aware vertex embeddings via concatenated structure- and text-based representations with a mutual attention mechanism, and provides experimental evidence (AUC gains on link prediction and comparable classification accuracy) on standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) against appropriate baselines. Mathematical components appear standard (negative sampling, Adam) and no incorrect derivations are indicated in the summary, but no formal proofs are required for the empirical contribution. The experimental methodology is broadly sound (relevant baselines and metrics), though the summary omits mention of statistical significance tests, ablations, or code release, which are minor gaps for reproducibility. Overall the results support the main claims, with only modest concerns about reporting completeness rather than fundamental technical flaws.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear main claims and provides experimental evidence showing significant link-prediction improvements and comparable node-classification performance on Cora, HepTh and Zhihu against reasonable baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE). However, from the summary there is no sign of ablation studies isolating the mutual-attention or concatenation design choices, no statistical significance testing, and no mention of released code or runtime/memory measurements to ensure reproducibility. Mathematical details appear minimal in the summary (no proofs), and some assumptions (e.g., benefit of concatenation vs. fusion) are not directly supported by controlled experiments, so the technical claims are plausible but not fully substantiated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear technical claims about producing context-aware vertex embeddings via concatenated structure- and text-based representations with a mutual attention mechanism, and provides experimental evidence (AUC gains on link prediction and comparable classification accuracy) on standard datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) against appropriate baselines. Mathematical components appear standard (negative sampling, Adam) and no incorrect derivations are indicated in the summary, but no formal proofs are required for the empirical contribution. The experimental methodology is broadly sound (relevant baselines and metrics), though the summary omits mention of statistical significance tests, ablations, or code release, which are minor gaps for reproducibility. Overall the results support the main claims, with only modest concerns about reporting completeness rather than fundamental technical flaws.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method and experiments, and its main contributions (context-aware embeddings, concatenation of structure and text embeddings, and mutual attention) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while related work coverage is adequate; figures and tables supporting AUC and classification accuracy comparisons seem informative. Writing quality is good overall with minimal ambiguity, though the absence of stated limitations and no mention of released code slightly weakens reproducibility-focused clarity for readers in my role. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation to method to experiments: CANE’s key idea (context-aware concatenated embeddings with a mutual attention mechanism) and optimization objectives are described early, and experiments report datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters. Notation and method description appear clear enough to follow (structure/text embeddings, mutual attention, negative sampling/Adam), and related work is adequately covered. The writing quality is reported as good, but some presentation details are missing in the summary (e.g., effectiveness of figures/tables and whether limitations or code release are shown), so a few minor clarity/presentation elements could be improved.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments cover multiple datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and compare to a reasonable set of baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) with reported hyperparameters and metrics (AUC, classification accuracy), which supports the core claims about improved link prediction. However, the summary does not indicate any ablation studies isolating the mutual-attention or concatenation design choices, nor are sensitivity analyses or runtime/memory measurements reported to assess practical costs. The paper also omits discussion of failure cases or limitations and does not mention released code, so reproducibility is only partially supported despite reported hyperparameters. | B(3): The paper provides reasonable experimental evidence across three datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and compares to multiple baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE), showing strong link-prediction gains and comparable vertex classification results, which supports the main claims to an extent. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported in the summary to isolate the contribution of the mutual-attention or the structure/text components, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, but code availability is not mentioned, which limits reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is adequate but misses important analyses and transparency to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover multiple datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and compare to a reasonable set of baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) with reported hyperparameters and metrics (AUC, classification accuracy), which supports the core claims about improved link prediction. However, the summary does not indicate any ablation studies isolating the mutual-attention or concatenation design choices, nor are sensitivity analyses or runtime/memory measurements reported to assess practical costs. The paper also omits discussion of failure cases or limitations and does not mention released code, so reproducibility is only partially supported despite reported hyperparameters.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides reasonable experimental evidence across three datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) and compares to multiple baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE), showing strong link-prediction gains and comparable vertex classification results, which supports the main claims to an extent. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported in the summary to isolate the contribution of the mutual-attention or the structure/text components, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, but code availability is not mentioned, which limits reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is adequate but misses important analyses and transparency to be fully convincing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper compares against a reasonable set of established network embedding baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on common datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) using standard metrics (AUC, classification accuracy). The evaluations appear fair (same data and metrics) and the paper honestly reports when prior methods perform comparably (vertex classification). However, from a reproducibility and completeness standpoint I would expect inclusion/discussion of more recent context- or attention-based graph representation methods and explicit runtime/memory comparisons; these omissions justify a score of 4 rather than 5. | B(4): The paper compares against a reasonable set of established baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec) and also includes text-aware competitors (TADW, CENE), uses the same datasets/metrics and reports hyperparameters, so comparisons appear fair. The authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (vertex classification) and present a clear analysis of how CANE’s mutual-attention text component differs from structure-only and text-augmented baselines. Coverage of related work is described as adequate, but the evaluation omits some more recent graph/text embedding approaches (e.g., newer GNN-based or advanced text-aware models), so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper compares against a reasonable set of established network embedding baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec, TADW, CENE) on common datasets (Cora, HepTh, Zhihu) using standard metrics (AUC, classification accuracy). The evaluations appear fair (same data and metrics) and the paper honestly reports when prior methods perform comparably (vertex classification). However, from a reproducibility and completeness standpoint I would expect inclusion/discussion of more recent context- or attention-based graph representation methods and explicit runtime/memory comparisons; these omissions justify a score of 4 rather than 5.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of established baselines (DeepWalk, LINE, node2vec) and also includes text-aware competitors (TADW, CENE), uses the same datasets/metrics and reports hyperparameters, so comparisons appear fair. The authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (vertex classification) and present a clear analysis of how CANE’s mutual-attention text component differs from structure-only and text-augmented baselines. Coverage of related work is described as adequate, but the evaluation omits some more recent graph/text embedding approaches (e.g., newer GNN-based or advanced text-aware models), so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: CANE learns context-aware embeddings for vertices, producing different embeddings for a vertex depending on its interaction context.
CANE combines structure-based embeddings and text-based embeddings (concatenated) and uses a mutual attention mechanism to model semantic relationships between vertices.

CANE optimizes objectives for both structure and text, employs negative sampling and Adam for training, and achieves significant improvements on link prediction and comparable performance on vertex classification compared to state-of-the-art methods.
_summary.methodology: CANE represents each vertex with a concatenation of a structure-based embedding and a text-based embedding, where the text embedding can be context-aware via a mutual attention mechanism; the model optimizes structure- and text-based objectives with negative sampling and Adam.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating graph representation learning systems in industry and academia; expertise in experimental design, optimization, and software reproducibility.
traits: checks for clear descriptions of training procedures (hyperparameters, initialization, optimizer settings, negative sampling details)
expects ablation studies that isolate contributions of model components (e.g., mutual attention, concatenation vs. fusion)
looks for robust evaluation: multiple datasets, statistical significance, strong and well-tuned baselines
values reproducibility artifacts (pseudo-code, complexity analysis, code or seed-level guidance) and runtime/memory measurements
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether the experiments and methodology convincingly support the claims and whether the work can be reliably reproduced and compared to baselines.

reviewer_b.name: Graph Representation Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher specializing in network embedding and relational learning with a track record of publishing on context-dependent and text-enhanced graph models.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work on context-aware embeddings, attention mechanisms, and text-augmented graph representations
values clear articulation of the conceptual contribution and how it advances theory or practice in network representation learning
notices overlaps with and distinctions from closely related methods (e.g., neighbor-attention, personalized embeddings, graph neural networks incorporating text)
cares about broader impact: applicability to real-world graph problems, scalability, and limitations
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I evaluate the significance and originality of the idea, how it fits into the literature, and whether the contribution meaningfully moves the field forward.
